nativeRange.habitat: Shallow tropical reef flats, reef crests, and intertidal zones; tolerates variable salinity and high water movement; characteristic of reef-building zones in the Indo-Pacific and Caribbean

decorPreferences: stable rock base for encrusting growth

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via zooxanthellae. In the aquarium, Porites generally does not require target feeding in the way LPS corals do; however, microplankton and amino acid supplementation have been documented to support growth and coloration. The 'Christmas Tree Rock' form (Porites colonized by Spirobranchus giganteus worms) benefits from supplemental liquid plankton-based foods to sustain the filter-feeding worms.

goodWith: peaceful SPS corals with adequate spacing
avoidWith: corals placed in direct contact (sweeper tentacle damage)
copper-based medications (lethal to Christmas Tree Worms if present)
aggressive fish that may nip polyps

summary: Porites is a genus of massive and encrusting SPS reef-building corals renowned for hardiness among stony corals. Some colonies host Spirobranchus giganteus worms, sold as 'Christmas Tree Rock.' Advanced care; requires stable high-flow, high-light reef conditions.
careNotes:
Note: Porites is a large genus (100+ described species) of stony corals spanning a wide range of growth forms — massive boulders, encrusting sheets, and branching finger forms. In the aquarium trade, most specimens are sold at the genus level (Porites sp.) as care requirements are broadly consistent. The scientificName is set to 'Porites sp.' to reflect this trade convention. Common aquarium species include P. lobata, P. lutea, P. cylindrica, and P. porites.

Porites are among the hardiest stony (SPS) corals available in the hobby — significantly more forgiving than Acropora, and widely considered more resilient than most other SPS. ReefBuilders has documented wild colonies surviving accidental freshwater influx events. However, they still demand the stable, chemistry-driven conditions of an established reef system and should not be treated as beginner corals.

• Care level note: Hobby sources (LiveAquaria, ReefChasers) commonly rate Porites as 'moderate.' Scientific literature (Wikipedia/CORAL Magazine) rates them 'very difficult' due to strict wild-collection survival rates. 'Advanced' is used here as a calibrated middle ground — acknowledging that aquacultured Porites frags are hardier than wild-collected specimens but still require an established reef system with precise chemistry.

• Lighting: High intensity is generally recommended — approximately 150–300 PAR at the colony surface. ReefChasers indicates 200–250 PAR as optimal, with up to 350 PAR tolerated. Acclimate new specimens gradually, starting lower in the tank and moving upward over 2–4 weeks.

• Flow: Moderate to high, indirect flow is generally advised. Avoid direct laminar flow pointed at the colony; polyps should visibly sway. High import/high export filtration systems appear to benefit nutrient uptake.

• Placement: Upper to middle rockwork is generally recommended, though Porites are naturally found from intertidal zones (high light) to 40 m depth (lower light), so there is more placement flexibility than with Acropora. Encrusting and massive growth forms tend to do well on open rock surfaces.

• Chemistry: Maintain calcium (generally around 400–450 ppm), alkalinity (8–11 dKH), and magnesium (1250–1350 ppm) in the typical reef range. Porites appears more tolerant of minor parameter swings than Acropora, but stability remains important for long-term health and color.

• Aggression: Porites have very short sweeper tentacles and are considered peaceful among corals. Maintain at least 2–5 cm spacing from neighboring corals to prevent chemical warfare (allelopathy) and physical contact.

• Growth: In the wild, Porites colonies can become enormous — the widest recorded colony (Great Barrier Reef, 2021) measured 10.4 meters across and was estimated to be over 400 years old. In home aquaria, captive colonies are far smaller; encrusting frags typically expand 2–5 cm per year under good conditions.

• Christmas Tree Rock (Spirobranchus giganteus symbiosis): Several Porites species — especially P. lobata and P. lutea — are natural hosts for Christmas Tree Worms (Spirobranchus giganteus), a polychaete tube worm with colorful double-spiral radiole crowns. Wild Porites heads colonized by these worms are collected and sold as 'Christmas Tree Rock' or 'Christmas Tree Worm Rock,' a popular live-rock display item. The worm burrows into the coral skeleton when it is a larva, and the coral tissue grows over the tube — the worm and coral become permanently intertwined. If the Porites host coral dies, the Christmas Tree Worms will shortly follow (they cannot survive without a living coral host to anchor their tubes). Maintaining a Christmas Tree Rock therefore requires meeting all Porites husbandry requirements AND providing supplemental liquid microplankton/phytoplankton foods for the filter-feeding worms. The worms are sensitive to copper-based medications, which will kill them rapidly. LiveAquaria rates Christmas Tree Worm Rock as 'Expert Only,' and this combined system is considerably more demanding than plain Porites frags.

• Fragmentation: Porites can be fragged with bone cutters; encrusting forms also propagate readily by simple branchlet separation or rubble attachment.
breedingNotes: Fragmentation is the primary propagation method in captivity. Encrusting and branching Porites frags heal and attach readily to frag plugs or rubble. Sexual reproduction in the wild occurs via broadcast spawning, typically synchronized with lunar cycles, but captive sexual breeding is not reported in the hobby. Christmas Tree Worms (Spirobranchus giganteus) hosted by Porites do reproduce sexually; larvae are broadcast spawned and must settle on a living coral host, making captive worm reproduction very rare.

primary.name: LiveAquaria – Yellow Porites Coral
primary.notes: Primary source for water parameters (temperature 72-78°F, pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.023-1.025, kH 8-12), lighting (high intensity T-5/LED/halide), care level (moderate), diet (zooxanthellae + microplankton supplement), and temperament (peaceful). Also sourced Christmas Tree Worm Rock page for worm-hosting symbiosis and Expert Only designation.

additional.name: ReefChasers – Porites Coral Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters: temperature 77-84°F, pH 8.0-8.4, kH 8-9, calcium 400-450 ppm, PAR 200-350 (optimal 200-250). Care level rated moderate. Flow moderate to high; peaceful temperament. Indo-Pacific native range confirmed. Temperature averaged with LiveAquaria: min 23°C, max 27°C. kH upper bound averaged: 8-11.

additional.name: Wikipedia – Porites
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (Family: Poritidae, Order: Scleractinia), native range (Indo-Pacific, Caribbean, Great Barrier Reef), depth range, Christmas Tree Worm (Spirobranchus giganteus) hosting relationship, and wild biology. Wikipedia characterizes captive care as 'very difficult' — higher difficulty than hobby retail sources; careLevel set to 'advanced' as calibrated middle ground.

additional.name: Wikipedia – Spirobranchus giganteus
additional.notes: Used to document the Christmas Tree Worm symbiosis: worm settles on living Porites and is overgrown by coral tissue; worm is a filter feeder using radioles; range spans Caribbean to Indo-Pacific; worm cannot survive without living Porites host.

additional.name: ReefBuilders – Fiji Yellow Porites
additional.notes: Secondary confirmation of hardiness ('some of the hardiest stony corals'), moderate to high flow preference, Fiji/Indo-Pacific origin, and successful fragmentation in captivity. No numerical parameters provided.
